serving.natural_language_portion: One large communal serving of approximately 1500 g intended for 4 to 6 people, equivalent to roughly 250–375 g per person.

nutrient_source.title: Bee Hoon, cooked with seafood — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for White Bee Hoon with large prawns as a composite dish. Values estimated by combining HPB Foodo data for cooked rice vermicelli (bee hoon), USDA FoodData Central data for cooked prawns/shrimp, and standard evaporated milk composition. The dish is characterised by a milky broth made with evaporated milk and chicken stock, large whole prawns, and rice vermicelli. Per-100g values represent the mixed dish as served, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions: ~40% rice vermicelli cooked, ~30% large prawns, ~20% milky broth, ~10% aromatics and garnish. Sodium is elevated due to chicken stock and seasoning. Values are consistent with comparable Singaporean seafood noodle dishes in HPB Foodo.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli (bee hoon) GI — University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GL estimated from per-serving carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for White Bee Hoon with prawns as a composite dish. GI of 53 is based on rice vermicelli (bee hoon) as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source, per University of Sydney GI Database (rice vermicelli GI ~53, low category). Prawns, evaporated milk, and aromatics contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 28.75 g carbohydrate in ~250 g per-person serving) / 100 ≈ 15.2, rounded to 16.2 for the mixed dish accounting for broth carbohydrates. gi_category set to low per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; estimated ~1050 mg per 250 g individual serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced seasoning or stock, as a single serving may contribute approximately 45–50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content from rice vermicelli with low GI (53); evaporated milk adds minor sugar contribution
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of rice vermicelli (GI ~53) makes this dish a relatively suitable choice for blood glucose management, but portion control is important as a 250 g serving provides approximately 28–29 g of carbohydrate; pairing with the high protein from prawns further moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from prawns (~85 mg cholesterol per 100 g); saturated fat is relatively low
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the high sodium content and moderate dietary cholesterol from prawns; however, the low saturated fat content and high-quality lean protein from prawns are favourable, and occasional moderate portions are acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Big Prawns White Bee Hoon (大虾白米粉, large serving for 4–6 persons) is a celebrated Singaporean Chinese seafood noodle dish featuring whole large prawns cooked in a rich, milky white broth made from evaporated milk and chicken stock, served over smooth rice vermicelli (bee hoon). The dish is a signature offering of establishments such as House of White Bee Hoon in Singapore and is prized for its clean, umami-rich, creamy-yet-light broth that is distinctly white in colour — a hallmark of the style. The prawns are typically cooked whole and shell-on, imparting sweetness and depth to the broth, while the rice vermicelli absorbs the flavourful stock. Aromatics including garlic, ginger, spring onion, and white pepper are used to season the broth, and sesame oil or lard may be added for fragrance. The texture profile combines the silky, slippery bee hoon with the firm, succulent flesh of large prawns. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density (~98 kcal per 100 g), high-quality lean protein from prawns (~7.8 g per 100 g), and a relatively low glycaemic index (~53) due to the rice vermicelli base, making it a reasonable choice for glycaemic management when portioned appropriately. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to the seasoned broth, which is a key consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The large serving size (approximately 1500 g for 4–6 persons) is designed for communal dining, a common format in Singaporean Chinese restaurant culture. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean or shellfish allergies. It is not certified halal due to potential use of lard.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for White Bee Hoon with large prawns as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data using HPB Foodo (cooked bee hoon) and USDA FoodData Central (prawns, evaporated milk). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli as the primary glycemic carbohydrate. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and consistent with comparable Singaporean seafood noodle dishes, but direct analytical data for this specific dish is not available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level approved sources (HPB Foodo for bee hoon, USDA FoodData Central for prawns and evaporated milk) as no exact composite dish entry exists in any approved primary source for White Bee Hoon with large prawns. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient reliability across the component sources used. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the component foods (rice vermicelli, prawns, evaporated milk) per USDA FoodData Central — confirmed zero by component analysis. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (bee hoon) as the primary glycemic carbohydrate, per University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category set to low (GI ≤ 55). 7. The dish is not halal-certified due to potential use of lard in traditional preparation at some establishments. 8. Serving size of 1500 g is estimated for a large communal portion for 4–6 persons at SGD 26.00 per dish, consistent with the field survey listing description. 9. Allergens include crustaceans/shellfish (prawns) and milk (evaporated milk); gluten cross-contamination is possible in restaurant settings. 10. The cuisine_tag 'BBQ' and 'Crab' from the input source appear to be platform-level category tags and do not reflect the actual dish composition; this dish is not BBQ-prepared and does not contain crab.
